text.title: llamaindex-chat-engine Core Rules
text.summary: Rules for selecting LlamaIndex chat-engine mode, bounding memory, and wiring streaming.

rule.statement: Default to chat_mode='condense_plus_context' for document-grounded multi-turn Q&A. It rewrites the follow-up question with conversation context before retrieval.
rule.rationale: condense_plus_context handles 'tell me more about that' follow-ups without needing tool use.

rule.statement: Use chat_mode='react' only when tool calls are required. ReAct adds 2-3 extra LLM calls per turn vs condense_plus_context.
rule.rationale: ReAct mode is expensive; reserve it for genuine multi-tool reasoning.

rule.statement: Bound conversation memory with ChatMemoryBuffer where token_limit is roughly half the context window. For an 8k context model, set token_limit=3900.
rule.rationale: Half the context window leaves room for retrieved chunks and the current question.

rule.statement: Instantiate a per-session ChatMemoryBuffer; never share a global instance across users.
rule.rationale: A shared buffer leaks history across users — same failure pattern as [[langchain-memory]] rule r13.

rule.statement: Enable streaming for chat UI; use chat_engine.stream_chat() not .chat().
rule.rationale: Perceived latency drops with first-token streaming; the difference for a chatbot is qualitative.

rule.statement: If a post-processor formats the answer after generation, choose either stream-and-no-postprocess OR no-stream-and-postprocess. Never both.
rule.rationale: Streaming + post-process produces UI flicker where the half-streamed answer jumps to the formatted final.

rule.statement: Pin the context_prompt template explicitly; never rely on LlamaIndex defaults for production. The defaults shift between minor releases.
rule.rationale: Default prompt changes have silently regressed user-facing answers across upgrades.

rule.statement: Verify session_id is a server-generated UUID, never client-supplied. Same rule as conversation memory across frameworks.
rule.rationale: Predictable session IDs let an attacker fetch another user's chat history.
